data.title: Assessing Marten Transport (MRTN) Valuation After Recent Share Price Weakness
data.description:
Advertisement

What Marten Transport’s recent performance might mean for investors

Marten Transport (MRTN) has come onto investors’ radar after a period where the share price showed a 2.5% gain over the past day, in contrast with negative moves over the past week and month.

See our latest analysis for Marten Transport.

That 2.5% one day share price gain comes after a 14.2% 30 day share price decline and a 12.5% 1 year total shareholder return loss, which suggests recent momentum has been fading rather than building.

If Marten’s latest move has you reassessing the transport space, it could be a good moment to broaden your search with our screener of 18 top founder-led companies.

With Marten Transport trading at $11.75 and analyst targets at $14.00, along with mixed recent returns, the key question is whether the stock is already fairly valued or if the market is underestimating its future growth potential.

Price-to-Earnings of 53.6x: Is it justified?

Marten Transport is trading on a P/E of 53.6x, which, set against recent share price weakness and mixed fundamentals, suggests investors are paying a rich price for current earnings.

The P/E ratio compares the share price with earnings per share, so a higher multiple typically means the market is placing a higher value on each dollar of profit. For a trucking and logistics business, that usually reflects expectations for stronger profitability ahead or confidence that current earnings do not reflect its long term earning power.

In Marten Transport's case, the picture is mixed. On one hand, its P/E of 53.6x is below the peer average of 63.7x, which points to comparatively lower optimism than some direct peers. On the other hand, the same 53.6x multiple is well above the broader US Transportation industry average of 33.7x and considerably above the estimated fair P/E of 24.5x. This implies the current market pricing is far richer than the level our model suggests the multiple could move toward if expectations cooled.

However, a 53.6x P/E, together with a 12.5% 1-year total return loss and a 39.5% 3-year total return loss, could limit enthusiasm if sentiment weakens further.

Another view from our DCF model

With the share price at $11.46 and our estimate of future cash flow value at $6.79, the stock screens as overvalued on this method too. So how comfortable are you paying that kind of premium?

data.title: Is It Time To Reassess California Resources (CRC) After Its Recent Share Price Swings?
data.description:
• If you are looking at California Resources and wondering whether the current share price reflects its true worth, you are not alone.
  • The stock closed at US$61.47 recently, after a 5.1% decline over the last 7 days, an 8.3% gain over 30 days, and returns of 32.5% year to date and 45.6% over the last year.
  • These moves have come as investors continue to track company specific developments and broader energy sector sentiment, with recent attention on how producers are positioning themselves within California's regulatory and operational backdrop. This context helps explain why the market has been reassessing risk and return expectations around the stock.
  • On our valuation checks, California Resources scores 3 out of 6, as shown in its valuation summary. Next, we will compare different valuation approaches to that score while also pointing to a more complete way to think about value at the end of this article.

California Resources delivered 45.6% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Oil and Gas industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: California Resources Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth today by projecting the cash it could generate in the future and then discounting those amounts back to a single present value figure.

For California Resources, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flows reported and projected in US$. The company’s latest twelve month free cash flow is about US$582.3 million. Analysts provide explicit free cash flow estimates out to 2028, including US$442.9 million for 2026 and US$415.5 million for 2027, with US$402.5 million for 2028. Beyond that, Simply Wall St extrapolates cash flows through 2035, and all of those cash flows are discounted back to today.

Combining these discounted cash flows produces an estimated intrinsic value of about US$107.36 per share. Compared with the recent share price of US$61.47, the model suggests California Resources trades at roughly a 42.7% discount, which points to the stock being undervalued on this DCF view.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests California Resources is undervalued by 42.7%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 47 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for California Resources.

Approach 2: California Resources Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like California Resources, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of earnings. A higher or lower P/E often reflects what the market is pricing in for future growth and how much risk investors feel they are taking on.

In simple terms, stronger expected growth and lower perceived risk tend to support a higher “normal” P/E, while lower growth expectations or higher risk usually mean a lower one. California Resources currently trades on a P/E of 15.00x, compared with the Oil and Gas industry average of 15.37x and a much higher peer group average of 46.93x.

Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary “Fair Ratio” for the company of 14.29x. This is designed to be more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison because it blends in company specific factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, risk profile, industry, and market cap. By using these inputs together, the Fair Ratio aims to answer what a more company appropriate P/E should look like.

Set against this Fair Ratio of 14.29x, the current P/E of 15.00x suggests California Resources screens as slightly overvalued on this metric.

Result: OVERVALUED

With Narratives, you spell out your story for California Resources, link it to explicit forecasts for revenue, earnings and margins, and arrive at your own Fair Value that you can compare with the current price on Simply Wall St’s Community page. All of this then updates automatically as fresh news and earnings arrive. Different investors can look at the same company and reasonably land on very different fair values, such as around US$48 based on more cautious assumptions or closer to US$73 based on a more optimistic view of carbon capture and earnings. They can then decide whether the current share price looks high, low, or about right for their chosen story.

For California Resources, however, we will make it really easy for you with previews of two leading California Resources Narratives:

First up is a more optimistic angle that leans into carbon management, permitting reforms, and capital returns.

🐂 California Resources Bull Case

Fair value in this narrative: US$63.31 per share

Implied discount to this fair value at the recent price of US$61.47: about 2.9% undervalued

Forecast annual revenue growth used in this narrative: 18.29%

  • This narrative assumes California regulatory reforms and support for in-state energy and CCS give California Resources more room to work through its inventory and generate carbon management revenue alongside oil and gas.
  • It also builds in stronger margins and free cash flow over time, helped by cost discipline, merger synergies, and share count reduction, which lifts earnings per share in the model.
  • It arrives at a fair value that is modestly above the recent share price, with the view that policy support, CCS progress, and an improved earnings mix justify that gap.

On the other side is a more cautious view that leans into regulatory risk, renewables pressure, and margin compression.

🐻 California Resources Bear Case

Fair value in this narrative: US$55.10 per share

Implied premium to this fair value at the recent price of US$61.47: about 11.6% overvalued

Forecast annual revenue growth used in this narrative: 9.46%

  • This narrative flags the risk that faster adoption of renewables, tighter climate policy, and concentrated exposure to California could weigh on long-term oil and gas revenues and keep compliance costs high.
  • It treats CCS and clean power projects as capital intensive and still unproven in the state, with potential for weaker returns if approvals, demand, or pricing fall short of expectations.
  • It uses lower profit margin assumptions and a higher future P/E multiple to arrive at a fair value below the recent share price, suggesting the stock could be pricing in more optimism than this scenario supports.

Taken together, these two narratives frame a clear range for you to think about California Resources, from a modest premium to current pricing to a modest discount. Over recent periods the company has seen quarterly revenue move from €182.5 million in Q4 2024 to €437.1 million in Q4 2025, while quarterly EPS shifted from €0.34 to €0.77. This sets up a results season where investors are likely to focus on how sustainable these earnings are against softer net profit margins.

See our full analysis for Grenke.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the prevailing narratives around Grenke's growth potential, risk profile, and profitability trajectory.

See what the community is saying about Grenke

Advertisement

Margins Under Pressure At 7.5%

  • Over the last 12 months, Grenke earned €69.8 million on €924.3 million of revenue, which works out to a 7.5% net profit margin compared with 11.3% in the prior year.
  • Consensus narrative supporters point out that this lower margin sits alongside digitalisation efforts and new-market expansion, yet:
    • Trailing earnings still rose 6.6% over the year, so profitability and margin trends are telling slightly different stories.
    • Ongoing restructuring and higher risk costs are cited as headwinds, and the current 7.5% margin illustrates how those costs are flowing through to the bottom line.

Quarterly Profit Swing Across 2025

  • Within FY 2025, net income moved from a small loss of €0.7 million in Q1 to €33.9 million in Q4, while quarterly revenue ranged from €155.0 million to €437.1 million and EPS went from a slight loss to €0.77.
  • Bulls argue that cost discipline and digitalisation can support higher long term margins, and this pattern in 2025 interacts with that story in a few ways:
    • The move from a loss in Q1 to positive net income and EPS in later quarters fits with the bullish view that profitability can improve as efficiency gains build.
    • At the same time, the wide spread between early year results and Q4 highlights how sensitive earnings still are to costs and credit expenses, which bulls need to keep in mind.

Bulls say Grenke's sharp profit recovery through 2025 could be the early sign of the earnings ramp they expect. However, the step up from a Q1 loss to a strong Q4 also shows how dependent that story is on keeping costs and risk charges in check. 🐂 Grenke Bull Case

Cheap P/E, But Cash Flow Strain

  • With the share price at €13.26, Grenke trades on a P/E of 8.4x and about 34.5% below a DCF fair value of €20.24, while net profit margins are 7.5% and debt and the 3.02% dividend are not well covered by operating and free cash flows.
  • Bears focus on these weaker cash flow signals and margin compression, and the current numbers give them some support:
    • The gap between the 7.5% margin and last year’s 11.3% margin aligns with concerns that higher risk costs and expenses are eating into profitability.
    • The fact that both debt and the dividend are not well covered by cash flows shows why some investors worry that a low P/E and discount to DCF fair value might reflect real balance sheet and payout constraints.

Skeptics point out that a low 8.4x P/E and discount to DCF fair value mean less if cash flows are struggling to support debt and dividends. This is exactly the pressure they see in Grenke’s latest numbers. 🐻 Grenke Bear Case
